title: New Analytical Models for Flow Induced by Pumping in a Stream-Aquifer System: A New Robin Boundary Condition Reflecting Joint Effect of Streambed Width and Storage

research.question: How can a stream-aquifer pumping model retain the joint transient effects of streambed width and storage without explicitly meshing a finite streambed zone?
research.system: A constant-rate well in a homogeneous confined aquifer beside a straight partially penetrating stream, represented either by a single aquifer with a lagging Robin boundary or by coupled aquifer and finite-width streambed zones.
research.inputData: Analytical parameter scenarios, finite-element benchmarks, and stream-depletion observations from the Doyleston Drain pumping experiment in New Zealand.
research.method: Derive transformed drawdown and stream-depletion solutions for the lagging-boundary and two-zone representations, equate their cumulative lag and streambed-storage terms to identify the lag time, and calibrate streambed parameters to field SDR data.
research.benchmark: The two analytical models are compared with each other, Hunt's nonlagging Robin solution, Glover and Balmer's Dirichlet solution, finite-element simulations, and Doyleston Drain measurements.
research.uncertainty: Sensitivity coefficients quantify parameter influence, while field-fit SEE compares models. In the field case, the inferred lag may include aquifer storage beneath the drain because the full-penetration approximation is not satisfied.
research.conclusion: Within the paper's confined homogeneous geometry and tested parameter cases, the lagging Robin boundary is an efficient surrogate whose SDR difference from the explicit streambed model depends on w_D beta, well distance, and pumping time.

narrative.throughline: Replace an explicitly resolved, storage-bearing streambed with a delayed boundary flux, prove the parameter link, and test whether the compact boundary preserves stream depletion.
narrative.audienceStartingPoint: Pumping beside a stream draws water from aquifer storage and eventually from stream filtration.
narrative.stakes: Ignoring streambed width and storage shifts the timing and magnitude of stream depletion used for ecological and water-allocation decisions.
narrative.resolution: A lag term equal to half the squared streambed width divided by streambed diffusivity carries the missing storage physics into a single-zone Robin boundary.

background.premise: Stream depletion rate links pumping to the fraction of well discharge supplied by stream filtration.
background.visibleEvidence: A pumping cone reaches a stream and redirects a portion of streamflow through the bed toward the well.
background.whyNow: That fraction is a direct indicator of pumping impact on the connected stream.
background.boundary: SDR is a rate ratio, not the stream-stage response or total ecological effect.
background.sourceExcerpt: (SDR), deﬁned as the ratio of a stream water ﬁltration rate to a well discharge rate, can be used in
background.visualObject: stream, clogged bed, pumping well, and partitioned flow arrows
background.motionPurpose: Trace the growing contribution of stream filtration to well discharge.
background.stepDetail: Animate aquifer-storage water first and stream-derived water later, with SDR as their rate ratio.
background.transitionBridge: Inspect the boundary law that controls passage through the streambed.

background.premise: The conventional Robin boundary omits the transient effects of streambed storage and finite width.
background.visibleEvidence: A zero-thickness boundary replaces a visibly thick, water-storing streambed layer.
background.whyNow: The omitted storage can delay stream filtration even after the drawdown cone reaches the streambed.
background.boundary: The correction targets a clogged streambed in a confined stream-aquifer setting.
background.sourceExcerpt: excludes a term associated with streambed accounting for the effects of streambed storage and width. This
background.visualObject: conventional boundary beside a finite-width storage layer
background.motionPurpose: Expose the physical volume erased by the standard boundary idealization.
background.stepDetail: Collapse the streambed layer to a line and show its storage reservoir disappearing.
background.transitionBridge: Restore that reservoir either explicitly or through a lag term.

background.premise: The paper compares a lagging-boundary single-zone model with an explicit finite-width two-zone model.
background.visibleEvidence: The same stream-aquifer cross-section forks into a boundary-only representation and a resolved streambed zone.
background.whyNow: The paired models permit a physical interpretation of the lag parameter and a direct accuracy-cost comparison.
background.boundary: Both models assume homogeneous isotropic confined flow and a constant stream water level; the stream may be treated as fully penetrating only under the stated geometry criteria.
background.sourceExcerpt: Panel (b) regards the system as a single‐zone aquifer with the stream treated as a lagging Robin boundary condition.
background.visualObject: paired single-zone and two-zone stream-aquifer schematics
background.motionPurpose: Hold external geometry fixed while changing only the streambed representation.
background.stepDetail: Split one schematic into panels b and c and align their pumping wells and stream edges.
background.transitionBridge: Write one governing representation for each panel.

methods.input: Single-zone aquifer drawdown with a time lag parameter at the clogged streambed.
methods.output: A boundary flux containing leakance and a drawdown-rate term.
methods.validity: The streambed is collapsed to infinitesimal width, and constant stream level is justified when filtration is small relative to streamflow.
methods.locator: PDF page 2, Introduction, Equation 2; PDF page 5, Equation 15
methods.sourceExcerpt: lag parameter denoted as τ in equation 1 to handle the time lag effect induced by the streambed. The new
methods.visualObject: boundary flux arrow responding continuously to drawdown and its time derivative
methods.motionPurpose: Make the derivative term visible as part of the instantaneous boundary relation.
methods.stepDetail: Plot boundary drawdown, attach its time derivative as a tangent slope, and combine drawdown with tau times that derivative into the filtration flux.
methods.transitionBridge: Resolve the same storage explicitly in the comparison model.

methods.input: A finite-width streambed adjoining the confined aquifer.
methods.output: Coupled streambed-aquifer equations with boundary and interface conditions.
methods.validity: A constant-head Dirichlet condition is imposed at the stream edge, and each homogeneous isotropic zone has its own transmissivity and storage.
methods.sourceExcerpt: The continuity requirements of drawdown and ﬂux at the streambed‐aquifer interface can be respectively denoted as
methods.visualObject: finite streambed strip with matched head and flux arrows at x=0
methods.motionPurpose: Assemble the two-zone equations and continuity conditions without displaying a solved response.
methods.stepDetail: Assign one flow equation to each zone, impose constant head at the stream edge, and join drawdown and flux at the interface.
methods.transitionBridge: The two formulated representations now enter their separate analytical derivations.

methods.input: Dimensionless single-zone drawdown retaining streamwise coordinate yD and the lagging Robin boundary
methods.output: Fourier-cosine-domain single-zone equations in xD and time
methods.validity: Symmetry at yD equals zero and decay at infinite streamwise distance support the cosine projection.
methods.sourceExcerpt: with the Fourier cosine parameter ω. The transformed model can then be written as
methods.visualObject: Single-zone streamwise drawdown profiles collapsing onto cosine modes
methods.motionPurpose: Remove the streamwise coordinate from the lagging-boundary model before transforming time.
methods.stepDetail: Project the yD dependence onto cosine parameter omega while retaining xD, time, and the derivative boundary term.
methods.transitionBridge: The projected single-zone modes remain time dependent for M04.

methods.output: An ordinary differential boundary problem in xD and Laplace parameter p
methods.validity: Zero initial drawdown and the transformed derivative boundary convert the time dependence into Laplace parameter p.
methods.sourceExcerpt: parameter p. The transformed model becomes an ordinary differential equation (ODE) with two bound-
methods.visualObject: Single-zone cosine modes entering the Laplace domain
methods.motionPurpose: Convert the projected temporal derivatives into an xD boundary-value problem.
methods.stepDetail: Map each time derivative to p, carry the zero initial state, and retain the transformed Robin derivative term.
methods.transitionBridge: The resulting single-zone ODE is solved in M05.

methods.input: Single-zone Laplace-Fourier ODEs with well jump and boundary conditions
methods.output: Laplace-Fourier drawdown expressions in Equations 21a and 21b
methods.validity: The solution enforces remote decay, the lagging Robin condition, and drawdown and flux continuity across the well location.
methods.locator: PDF page 15, Appendix A, Equations (A8)-(A12); PDF page 5, Equations (21a)-(21b)
methods.sourceExcerpt: Solving equations                   A8–A12b with replacing bsD by bsD;1
methods.visualObject: Two single-zone transformed profiles joined at the pumping-well coordinate
methods.motionPurpose: Obtain the transformed drawdown on both sides of the well location.
methods.stepDetail: Enforce the boundary and jump conditions and retain the two solved spectral expressions.
methods.transitionBridge: The solved single-zone spectra are inverted in time in M06.

methods.output: Time-domain Fourier cosine amplitudes for the single-zone aquifer
methods.validity: The inversion includes the reported branch point and pole of the transformed response.
methods.sourceExcerpt: With these relations, we can have the inverse Laplace transforms to bsD;1 and bsD;2, written as
methods.visualObject: Single-zone Laplace spectra unfolding into time-dependent cosine amplitudes
methods.motionPurpose: Restore pumping time while retaining the single-zone streamwise spectrum.
methods.stepDetail: Apply the branch-cut inversion and residue term to each transformed single-zone region.
methods.transitionBridge: The time-domain modes are reconstructed in physical space in M07.

methods.output: Physical-space single-zone drawdown in Equations 22a and 22b
methods.validity: The inverse cosine integrals reconstruct the yD dependence of the two xD regions.
methods.locator: PDF page 16, Appendix A, Equations (A15a)-(A15b); PDF page 5, Equations (22a)-(22b)
methods.sourceExcerpt: The formulas for the inverse Fourier cosine trans- form to equations A14a and A14b are expressed as
methods.visualObject: Single-zone cosine modes expanding into the physical drawdown field
methods.motionPurpose: Complete the separate single-zone derivation before turning to the explicit streambed model.
methods.stepDetail: Integrate the time-domain modes over omega and reveal the reconstructed single-zone field.
methods.transitionBridge: The same transform sequence is now applied to the two-zone representation.

methods.input: Dimensionless two-zone drawdown equations retaining the streamwise coordinate yD.
methods.output: Fourier-cosine-domain equations in xD time and spectral parameter omega.
methods.validity: The symmetry boundary at yD equals zero and remote decay permit a Fourier cosine projection of the two-zone model.
methods.sourceExcerpt: The former transform converts sD′ ðxD; yD; tDÞ into esD′ ðxD; ω; tDÞ and ∂2sD′ =∂y2D into −ω2es′D.
methods.visualObject: Streamwise drawdown profiles collapsing onto cosine modes
methods.motionPurpose: Remove the streamwise coordinate before treating time.
methods.stepDetail: Project yD dependence onto Fourier cosine parameter omega.
methods.transitionBridge: Each cosine mode remains time dependent.

methods.input: Time-dependent Fourier cosine modes in the streambed and aquifer.
methods.output: Laplace-Fourier ordinary differential equations in xD for the two-zone model.
methods.validity: Zero initial drawdown maps each time derivative directly to multiplication by Laplace parameter p.
methods.sourceExcerpt: The Laplace transform convertses′DðxD; ω; tDÞ intobsD′ ðxD; ω; pÞ
methods.visualObject: Cosine modes entering a Laplace p-plane
methods.motionPurpose: Remove time dependence after the spatial projection.
methods.stepDetail: Transform each projected time derivative into parameter p.
methods.transitionBridge: The resulting xD equations can be solved with interface conditions.

methods.input: Laplace-Fourier ordinary differential equations with streambed-aquifer interface conditions.
methods.output: Laplace-Fourier drawdown expressions for the streambed and both aquifer regions in Equations 25a through 25c.
methods.validity: The transformed solution enforces continuous drawdown and flux at xD equals zero plus the remote and stream-edge boundaries.
methods.locator: PDF page 17, Appendix C, Equations (C6)-(C9); PDF page 6, Equations (25a)-(25c)
methods.sourceExcerpt: Solving the model yields the Laplace‐domain solution, expressed as equations 25a–25c.
methods.visualObject: Three transformed xD profiles joined at the streambed interface
methods.motionPurpose: Obtain one transformed drawdown expression for each two-zone region.
methods.stepDetail: Solve the xD boundary-value problem for the three regions.
methods.transitionBridge: The solved spectra are ready to return to physical time.

methods.input: Laplace-Fourier drawdown expressions in Equations 25a through 25c.
methods.output: Time-domain Fourier cosine drawdown amplitudes for the streambed and aquifer regions.
methods.validity: The inversion formula accounts for the reported pole and branch point structure in the complex p plane.
methods.sourceExcerpt: The inverse Laplace transforms to equations 25a–25c are given below using the formula of equations A13a and A13b
methods.visualObject: Laplace spectra unfolding into time-dependent cosine amplitudes
methods.motionPurpose: Restore pumping time while retaining the streamwise spectrum.
methods.stepDetail: Apply the inverse Laplace formula to Equations 25a through 25c.
methods.transitionBridge: The time-dependent modes still require streamwise reconstruction.

methods.input: Time-domain Fourier cosine amplitudes for all two-zone regions.
methods.output: Physical-space analytical drawdown in the streambed and both aquifer regions in Equations 26a through 26c.
methods.validity: The inverse cosine integrals reconstruct the yD dependence from the amplitudes in Equations C10a through C10c.
methods.locator: PDF pages 17-18, Appendix C; PDF page 6, Equations (26a)-(26c)
methods.sourceExcerpt: Taking the inverse Fourier cosine transform to equations C10a–C10c leads to the analytical solution of drawdown, equations 26a–26c
methods.visualObject: Cosine modes expanding into the two-dimensional drawdown field
methods.motionPurpose: Restore streamwise position after time inversion.
methods.stepDetail: Apply inverse cosine reconstruction to the time-domain amplitudes.
methods.transitionBridge: The reconstructed boundary flux can now be aggregated into SDR.

methods.input: Time-dependent filtration flux along the stream boundary.
methods.output: Temporal stream depletion rate SDR for each model.
methods.validity: The streambed width and storage contribution is integrated along the infinite rectilinear stream using symmetry about the well-normal centerline.
methods.sourceExcerpt: the streambed width and storage on SDR as discussed in section 2.5. Note that equation 27 relies only on
methods.visualObject: distributed streambed flux arrows merging into one SDR gauge
methods.motionPurpose: Aggregate local filtration into the management quantity used for comparison.
methods.stepDetail: Sweep along the stream, collect each flux arrow, and normalize the sum by Q.
methods.transitionBridge: Equate the delayed boundary volume to explicit streambed storage.

methods.input: Cumulative lagged filtration water in the single-zone model and cumulative storage release in the two-zone streambed.
methods.output: The relation tau = w^2 S'/(2T'), equivalent to half the squared width divided by streambed hydraulic diffusivity.
methods.validity: The equivalence is derived from the long-time, zero-frequency terms of the two analytical representations.
methods.sourceExcerpt: The LHS represents the total quantity of ﬁltration water subject to the effect of time lag while the RHS accounts for the total quantity of water released from streambed storage.
methods.visualObject: two equal water-volume reservoirs labeled lag and storage
methods.motionPurpose: Give the abstract lag parameter a streambed width-storage interpretation.
methods.stepDetail: Fill both reservoirs from their respective models, balance them, and reveal tau beneath the equality sign.
methods.transitionBridge: Test the compact relation against explicit-zone and classical solutions.

methods.input: Drawdown or SDR response and one perturbed hydraulic parameter
methods.output: Normalized response sensitivity Si for each model parameter
methods.validity: Equation 33 approximates the normalized derivative with the reported increment of 10^-3 Pi.
methods.locator: PDF page 8, Section 2.5 Sensitivity Analysis, Equations (32)-(33); PDF pages 9-10, Figures 4-5
methods.sourceExcerpt: The normalized sensitivity coefﬁcient Si that evaluates change in drawdown or SDR in response to variation
methods.visualObject: One-at-a-time hydraulic perturbations producing normalized SDR sensitivity curves
methods.motionPurpose: Quantify how each hydraulic parameter changes the selected model response.
methods.stepDetail: Perturb one parameter by 10^-3 Pi, calculate the response difference, normalize it, and plot Si through time.
methods.transitionBridge: The sensitivity curves qualify the parameter-case comparison in M16.

methods.input: Analytical SDR curves over streambed leakance, width, storage, and well distance scenarios.
methods.output: Parameter-dependent SDR differences across the three tested w_D beta cases and well distances
methods.validity: The w_D beta threshold, pumping-time differences, and distance sensitivity belong to the paper's tested parameter scenarios rather than unconditional model equivalence.
methods.sourceExcerpt: as a Dirichlet boundary is also chosen. Cases 1–3 stand for wDβ = 10, 1, and 0.1, respectively, based on a ﬁxed
methods.visualObject: case-labeled SDR curves with w_D beta, pumping time, and well distance controls
methods.motionPurpose: Reveal how the reported model difference changes with the tested parameters.
methods.stepDetail: Set each reported w_D beta case, advance pumping time, vary well distance, and retain only the source-reported curve separations.
methods.transitionBridge: Carry the competing models to measured field depletion.

methods.input: Doyleston Drain SDR observations with aquifer T and S fixed from early drawdown.
methods.output: Estimated K'/w and tau or wS_s' values and model-specific SEE.
methods.validity: Only ten SDR observations constrain the fits; streambed width itself is not separately identifiable from the available data.
methods.locator: PDF page 11, Section 3.3, Table 2 and Figure 6
methods.sourceExcerpt: With the estimates of T and S, Table 2 displays K′/w and τ estimated by Solution 1, K′/w and wS′s by
methods.visualObject: field SDR points with three fitted curves and SEE labels
methods.motionPurpose: Confront the analytical representations with measured depletion.
methods.stepDetail: Reveal the observations first, then draw each calibrated curve and its SEE without extrapolating beyond the test.
methods.transitionBridge: Return the fitted lag to the physical streambed and expose the geometry caveat.

methods.input: Single-zone and two-zone domains with their reported finite-element boundaries and meshes
methods.output: Mesh-dependent steady-state SDR and computation time for both representations
methods.validity: Both finite-element models solve the corresponding flow equations with matched aquifer extent and the paper's distinct streambed boundaries.
methods.locator: PDF pages 12-13, Section 3.4 Computation Efficiency of Finite Element Solution, Figure 7
methods.sourceExcerpt: The ﬁnite element method is employed to solve the single‐zone aquifer and two‐zone aquifer models.
methods.visualObject: Coarse single-zone mesh beside a streambed-refined two-zone mesh
methods.motionPurpose: Produce the finite-element accuracy and timing records from the two model geometries.
methods.stepDetail: Apply each boundary set, refine the nodal mesh, solve steady-state SDR, and record computation time.
methods.transitionBridge: The paired finite-element records are compared for accuracy and efficiency in M19.

methods.input: Finite-element SDR values, nodal counts, and computation times for both models
methods.output: The reported accuracy and computing-time contrast at comparable SDR
methods.validity: The comparison uses steady-state SDR approaching unity and the paper's computing-time ratio TR at the nodal resolutions shown in Figure 7.
methods.locator: PDF page 13, Section 3.4 Computation Efficiency of Finite Element Solution, Figure 7
methods.sourceExcerpt: the two‐zone aquifer model takes nearly one order larger computing time than the other model.
methods.visualObject: Steady-state SDR accuracy points paired with nodal-count and computing-time-ratio readouts
methods.motionPurpose: Compare numerical accuracy and cost at the source-reported resolutions.
methods.stepDetail: Align the steady-state SDR values, display 2209 and 14655 nodal counts, and reveal TR near 10 at comparable SDR.
methods.transitionBridge: The efficiency result returns to the parameter-bounded analytical surrogate.

symbols.visualObject: ratio gauge fed by stream filtration and pumping arrows
symbols.meaning: stream filtration rate divided by pumping rate
symbols.transition: Local boundary fluxes merge into the SDR gauge.

symbols.visualObject: coefficient multiplying the boundary drawdown derivative
symbols.meaning: lag time in the Robin boundary
symbols.transition: The derivative coefficient becomes an equivalent streambed storage volume.

symbols.visualObject: streambed width bracket and storage-filled strip
symbols.meaning: physical relation between boundary lag, streambed width, storage, transmissivity, and hydraulic diffusivity
symbols.transition: The finite strip collapses into a boundary carrying tau.

symbols.meaning: lumped parameter for the joint streambed width and storage effect
symbols.transition: The slider controls separation among SDR curves.

return.visualObject: the original stream-aquifer cross-section with paired SDR curves that remain close or separate according to the tested w_D beta, pumping time, and well distance
return.claim: Under the paper's model and tested parameter scenarios, the lagging Robin boundary is a more efficient surrogate whose SDR remains close to, but not identical with, the explicit streambed-zone response; the reported differences depend on w_D beta, pumping time, and well distance.
return.claimBoundary: The comparison and the 0.1 threshold belong to the paper's confined, homogeneous, isotropic, constant-stage geometry and parameter cases. At Doyleston Drain, tau may also absorb aquifer storage beneath the drain because the aquifer thickness violates the full-penetration criterion.
return.locator: PDF page 11, Section 3.3; PDF pages 13-14, Section 4 Concluding Remarks
return.sourceExcerpt: streambed storage inﬂuence (Table 2). Note that the estimated τ in this experiment setting may reﬂect

visual.primaryObject: pumped confined aquifer beside a straight stream and clogged streambed
visual.accentObject: finite streambed strip transforming into a lagging Robin boundary
visual.measuredQuantity: drawdown and stream depletion rate SDR
visual.geometryNotes: Use Cartesian x normal to the stream and y along it, a well at distance d, streambed width w, and the paired single-zone/two-zone representations. Preserve the full-penetration criteria: aquifer thickness below the stream less than 3w and well-centerline distance divided by w greater than 10.
